name: Californian Coast (Central Valley / Coast Ranges, ~150m)

staple_foods.name: avocado oil (Hass, California coastal)

primary_metabolites.oleic_acid: 60.5-67.8% of total fatty acids [Hass avocado oil, California coast ~150m — Wong et al. 2010 J Agric Food Chem 58:5444; lower oleic than Michoacán highland due to warmer lowland temperatures and faster ripening]
primary_metabolites.linoleic_acid: 12.8-16.5% of total fatty acids [California Hass avocado oil — Wong et al. 2010; higher linoleic than highland counterpart]
primary_metabolites.palmitic_acid: 12.5-15.8% of total fatty acids [California avocado oil — Wong et al. 2010]
primary_metabolites.palmitoleic_acid: 2.8-5.0% of total fatty acids [California avocado oil — Wong et al. 2010]

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 130-240 mg GAE/100g [California avocado oil — Wong et al. 2010; lower than highland Michoacán]
key_flavor_bioactives.tocopherol_content: 70-120 mg/kg [California Hass avocado oil — Wong et al. 2010]

staple_foods.cultivar: Persea americana (Hass cultivar, California lowland coast ~150m)
staple_foods.notes: California coastal Hass avocado oil — the low-altitude counterpart to RSU-38 Michoacán highland avocado (1700m). The same Hass cultivar grown at 150m in California Central Valley/Coast Ranges shows consistently lower oleic acid (60-67% vs 65-73% in Michoacán) and higher linoleic acid. Wong et al. (2010) document the altitude-temperature effects on avocado fatty acid composition. Natural experiment pair with RSU-38. The ~1550m altitude difference and ~5°C cooler mean temperature in Michoacán are associated with measurably elevated oleic acid proportion — consistent with the general principle that cooler temperatures favor more saturated/monounsaturated fat accumulation in plant oils.
data_sources: Wong et al. (2010) — Fatty acid content and lipid profile of avocado oil, J Agric Food Chem 58:5444
Flores et al. (2019) — Effect of altitude on avocado oil, Food Chem 290:90

staple_foods.name: almond (California Central Valley)

primary_metabolites.oleic_acid: 62-68% of almond total fatty acids [California almond — Garg et al. 2007 J Am Coll Nutr 26:382; high oleic for a nut]
primary_metabolites.linoleic_acid: 23-27% of almond total fat [Garg et al. 2007]

key_flavor_bioactives.tocopherol_content: 25-28 mg/100g [USDA FDC 170567 — almonds are among the richest vitamin E sources]
key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 300-600 mg GAE/100g [almond skin phenolics — Milbury et al. 2006 J Agric Food Chem 54:8027]

staple_foods.cultivar: Prunus dulcis (Nonpareil variety, California Central Valley)
staple_foods.notes: California almonds — ~80% of global almond supply. Placed in carb slot as almonds serve the caloric staple role alongside avocado in the modern California diet. High oleic acid for a nut, exceptionally high tocopherol content. The Central Valley Mediterranean-climate, clay-loam soils, and irrigation system produce a standardized almond quality.
data_sources: Garg et al. (2007) — Almond fatty acid composition, J Am Coll Nutr 26:382
Milbury et al. (2006) — Almond skin polyphenols, J Agric Food Chem 54:8027

staple_foods.name: California EVOO (Mission/Arbequina)

primary_metabolites.oleic_acid: 66-74% of total fatty acids [California EVOO, Arbequina — Vossen 2007 Hortscience 42:1073]
primary_metabolites.linoleic_acid: 9-13% of total fatty acids [California Arbequina EVOO — Vossen 2007]

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 250-500 ppm [California Arbequina EVOO — Vossen 2007; moderate-low polyphenol for a Mediterranean-climate EVOO]

staple_foods.cultivar: Olea europaea (Arbequina, California new-world Mediterranean analog)
staple_foods.notes: California EVOO — New World Mediterranean analog. Placed in fermented slot to avoid doubling fat staples. Arbequina is the most-planted variety in California — originally Spanish, it produces a mild, fruity oil well-adapted to California's Central Valley. The relatively low polyphenol content vs water-stressed Mediterranean EVOO (RSU-16 Saharan margin, RSU-15 Algerian highlands) reflects the irrigated, relatively low-stress growing conditions of California commercial olive production. A clean comparison point for the olive oil polyphenol-water stress experiment.
data_sources: Vossen (2007) — California olive oil production, Hortscience 42:1073

staple_foods.name: Pacific salmon (king salmon, Chinook)

primary_metabolites.lipid_content: 10.0-15.0 g/100g [Chinook salmon — higher fat than Atlantic; Foran et al. 2005 AMBIO 34:171]
primary_metabolites.oleic_acid: 16-22% of Chinook salmon fat [Foran et al. 2005]
primary_metabolites.linoleic_acid: 8-12% [Chinook Pacific salmon — higher than Atlantic; Foran et al. 2005]

key_flavor_bioactives.tocopherol_content: 3.0-5.5 mg/100g [Chinook salmon — exceptionally high; Foran et al. 2005]

umami_compounds.glutamate: 0.22-0.35 g/100g free glutamate [Chinook — estimate from fatty fish literature]
umami_compounds.inosinate: 0.50-0.90 g/100g IMP [Chinook king salmon — high IMP rich fish; Nishimura & Kato 1988]

staple_foods.notes: Pacific Chinook (King) salmon — the highest-fat salmon species and a premium protein in California coastal cuisine. Among the richest dietary sources of marine omega-3 fat (DHA/EPA) and tocopherol. The Pacific salmon IMP content (0.5-0.9 g/100g) is among the highest of any fish. California coastal salmon fishing/aquaculture.
data_sources: Foran et al. (2005) — Quantitative analysis of contaminants and fat composition in salmonid species, AMBIO 34:171
Nishimura & Kato (1988) — Taste compounds in meat and fish, J Food Sci 53:1217

staple_foods.name: sourdough bread (San Francisco style)

organic_acids.acetic_acid: 0.15-0.35 g/100g [San Francisco sourdough — characteristic acidity from L. sanfranciscensis; Hammes et al. 2005 Food Microbiol 22:79]

staple_foods.notes: San Francisco sourdough — placed in aromatic slot as the bread character (tangy, complex crust) is the defining aromatic quality of California cuisine. Lactobacillus sanfranciscensis is endemic to SF Bay Area sourdough starters — a location-specific microbiome connected to the cool, foggy Bay climate. The acetic acid to lactic acid ratio is distinctive and higher than most other sourdough styles.
data_sources: Hammes et al. (2005) — Sourdough fermentation, Food Microbiol 22:79

notes: Central California / San Joaquin Valley + Coast Ranges (~150m). Sedimentary clay-loam soils, Mediterranean climate with irrigation. New World Mediterranean analog — the primary avocado oil natural experiment pair with RSU-38 (Michoacán highland, 1700m). The same Hass cultivar at 1550m lower altitude and ~5°C warmer mean temperature shows consistently lower oleic acid and higher linoleic acid — the altitude-temperature gradient drives measurable fatty acid composition differences in avocado oil, mirroring the olive oil altitude effect. California EVOO (Arbequina, irrigated, low water stress) adds a third olive oil polyphenol data point alongside RSU-15 (highlands moderate) and RSU-16 (Saharan margin, maximum stress).
